## Revenue Milestone Pages

### From $0 to $100 MRR: Your First Micro-SaaS Customers
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/milestones/0-to-100
- **Stage:** First Customers ($0-$100)
- **Summary:** The hardest revenue to earn. Once you cross $100 MRR, you've proven someone will pay for what you built. Here's how to get there without wasting months building the wrong thing.
- **Key Metrics:**
  - Total customers: 3-5 paying
  - Monthly churn: <15%
  - Time to first $100: 30-60 days
  - Support tickets/week: <5
  - Onboarding completion: >70%
- **Tactics:**
  - Pre-sell before you build (Low effort): Create a landing page describing your solution. Offer 50% lifetime discount to the first 5 customers. If 3 people pay before the product exists, build it.
  - Sell to your network (carefully) (Low effort): Reach out to former colleagues in your target industry. Frame it as: 'I'm building something, would love your feedback on this problem.' Don't ask for payment — ask for the problem.
  - Offer done-for-you service first (Medium effort): Instead of building automation, offer to do the task manually for $99. When you understand the workflow deeply, then build the automation. This funds your build phase.
  - One customer at a time (Medium effort): Don't try to scale yet. Find one customer, deliver exceptional value, ask for a testimonial, then find the next one. Manual outreach works at this stage.
- **Common Mistakes:** Building for 6 months without talking to a single customer; Pricing too low ($5-$10/month) — you can't validate demand at that price; Adding features before you have 5 paying customers; Focusing on SEO and ads instead of direct conversations
- **Time Estimate:** 30-60 days
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Should I charge from day one?
    A: Yes. Free users don't validate demand. Charging $19-$49/month from day one proves willingness to pay. If no one pays, you have the wrong idea, not the wrong price.
  - Q: How many customers do I need for $100 MRR?
    A: At $29/month, you need 4 customers. At $49/month, 2 customers. Higher prices mean faster validation. Don't underprice.

### From $100 to $500 MRR: Building Early Traction
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/milestones/100-to-500
- **Stage:** Early Traction ($100-$500)
- **Summary:** You have paying customers. Now you need more of them. This stage is about finding repeatable acquisition channels and improving your product based on real user feedback.
- **Key Metrics:**
  - Total customers: 10-20 paying
  - Monthly churn: <10%
  - Customer acquisition cost: <$20
  - Net promoter score: >30
  - Weekly active users: >50% of customers
- **Tactics:**
  - Ask every customer for referrals (Low effort): Send a personal email to each customer: 'If you know someone who would benefit from this, can I buy you coffee?' Offer a free month for referrals.
  - Write 10 blog posts targeting customer pain points (Medium effort): Each post should answer the exact question your customer typed into Google before they found you. Use Keyword research from the actual words your customers use.
  - Offer an annual plan at 2 months discount (Low effort): Convert monthly customers to annual. This improves retention AND gives you cash upfront. 'Pay $297/year instead of $29/month.'
  - Add a simple referral program (Medium effort): Give both parties 1 month free. Use a simple link. Track manually. Don't over-engineer it.
- **Common Mistakes:** Raising prices too fast before understanding willingness to pay; Adding features instead of improving the core workflow; Neglecting customer success — losing customers at $500 hurts more than at $100; Scaling paid ads before organic channels are proven
- **Time Estimate:** 2-4 months
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: How much should I spend on marketing at this stage?
    A: Close to $0. Every dollar goes to building. Organic content, direct outreach, and referrals are all free. Paid ads only make sense when you know your LTV:CAC ratio.
  - Q: Should I raise prices?
    A: Only if you're at capacity and customers are delighted. A 10-20% price increase on new customers is safe. Grandfather existing customers to avoid churn.

### From $500 to $1,000 MRR: Reaching Ramen Profitable
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/milestones/500-to-1k
- **Stage:** Ramen Profitable ($500-$1,000)
- **Summary:** You're past the fragile stage. Multiple customers, proven retention, and a product people use daily. Now it's about scaling what works and building the foundations for $4K/month.
- **Key Metrics:**
  - Monthly churn: <8%
  - Customer acquisition cost: <$50
  - Customer LTV: >$600
  - Organic traffic: >1,000 visits/month
  - Feature requests/feedback: >5/week
- **Tactics:**
  - Double down on your best acquisition channel (Medium effort): Look at where your last 10 customers came from. Is it blog posts? Reddit? Referrals? Do more of that. Kill channels that haven't produced a customer.
  - Run a price test (Medium effort): Split new signups: half at current price, half at +20%. Run for 1 month. If revenue per customer goes up more than conversion drops, raise prices.
  - Build 'aha moment' onboarding (High effort): Analyze your best customers: what did they do in their first week? Create an onboarding flow that gets every new user to that moment faster.
  - Write competitor comparison pages (Low effort): If competitors exist, write detailed comparison pages. 'X vs Y' pages capture high-intent traffic from people ready to switch or choose.
- **Common Mistakes:** 
- **Time Estimate:** 3-6 months
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: When should I hire help?
    A: Not yet. At $1,000 MRR, you can't afford an employee. But you can afford contractors: a freelancer for a one-time task, a VA for 5 hours/week.
  - Q: Should I add more payment options?
    A: Only if customers ask. Adding Paddle for EU customers or accepting bank transfers can reduce friction for specific segments.

### From $1,000 to $4,000 MRR: Reaching Your Freedom Number
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/milestones/1k-to-4k
- **Stage:** Freedom Number ($1,000-$4,000)
- **Summary:** This is the most important journey in micro-SaaS. Crossing $4,000 MRR transforms your side business from 'extra income' into 'I could quit my job' territory. The tactics change from survival to systems.
- **Key Metrics:**
  - Monthly churn: <5%
  - Customer count: 138+ (at $29/mo)
  - Organic traffic: >5,000 visits/month
  - Customer LTV: >$1,200
  - Support requests/week: <20 (tools should reduce this)
- **Tactics:**
  - SEO content engine (High effort): Build a content calendar with 4 posts/month targeting long-tail keywords. Use existing customer questions as blog topics. Aim for 10k organic visits/month.
  - Partner with complementary products (Medium effort): Find non-competing SaaS tools in adjacent niches. Offer their users a discount. Offer yours a discount on their product. Cross-promote via email.
  - Run a 'win-back' campaign for churned customers (Low effort): Export your churned customers. Send a personal email: 'I noticed you left. What would it take to get you back?' Offer a free month. You'll recover 5-10% of churned MRR.
  - Implement self-serve onboarding (High effort): Remove yourself from the funnel. Video onboarding, knowledge base, and automated email sequences should handle 90% of new signups.
- **Common Mistakes:** 
- **Time Estimate:** 6-12 months
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can I quit my job at $4,000 MRR?
    A: Yes, if your lifestyle aligns. $4,000/month covers core expenses for most single people in non-VHCOL areas. Factor in taxes ($4K MRR ≈ $3K after taxes) and health insurance ($300-$600/month).
  - Q: Should I raise prices at this stage?
    A: Grandfather existing customers. Raise prices for new customers by 20-30%. Your product is worth more now than when you started. Test it on a cohort.

### From $4,000 to $10,000 MRR: Full-Time Founder Mode
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/milestones/4k-to-10k
- **Stage:** Full Replacement ($4,000-$10,000)
- **Summary:** You've proven the model. Now it's about systems, leverage, and building a business that runs without you. At $10K MRR, you're a full-time founder — whether you've quit your job or not.
- **Key Metrics:**
  - Monthly churn: <4%
  - Customer count: 200+ (at $49/mo)
  - Organic traffic: >20K visits/month
  - Support tickets: <50/week (automated)
  - Team size: 1 FTE + contractors
- **Tactics:**
  - Hire your first contractor (Medium effort): Delegate support or content. Pay $500-$1K/month. Free up 10 hours/week for growth work. This is the most important investment you'll make.
  - Build an affiliate program (Medium effort): Offer 30% recurring commission. Existing customers and niche influencers can be your sales team. Automated signup and tracking.
  - Product-led growth loops (High effort): Add features that create virality: shareable reports, team invites, public dashboards. Each user brings more users.
- **Common Mistakes:** DIY-ing everything when you should delegate; Ignoring customer segments with different needs; Letting churn creep up as customer base grows
- **Time Estimate:** 6-12 months
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Should I form an S-Corp at this stage?
    A: Yes. At $10K+ MRR ($120K+ ARR), an S-Corp election saves you self-employment tax. Talk to a CPA about the election timing.
  - Q: How do I handle customer support at scale?
    A: Hire a part-time support person. Create a knowledge base that answers the top 20 questions. Automate responses for common issues.

### From $10,000 to $25,000 MRR: Serious Business
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/milestones/10k-to-25k
- **Stage:** Scaling ($10,000-$25,000)
- **Summary:** Your micro-SaaS is now a real company. At $25K MRR ($300K ARR), you're in the top 5% of bootstrapped SaaS businesses. The game changes from 'survive' to 'systematize.'
- **Key Metrics:**
  - Monthly churn: <3%
  - Customer count: 500+ (at $49/mo)
  - Organic growth: >70% of new customers
  - Team size: 2-3 FTE
  - Gross margin: >80%
- **Tactics:**
  - Expand to adjacent markets (High effort): Your product serves one niche. Expand to a related niche with similar workflow. The code is 80% the same; the messaging is different.
  - Enterprise features for higher tiers (High effort): Add SSO, role-based access, audit logs, and SLA support. Charge $199-$499/month. Enterprise customers are stickier and more valuable.
  - SEO content at scale (Medium effort): Publish 8+ articles/month. Target 'best X for Y', 'X vs Y', and 'X alternative' keywords. Build topical clusters for domain authority.
- **Common Mistakes:** Not differentiating between user segments; Under-investing in brand and positioning; Keeping prices too low for enterprise value delivered
- **Time Estimate:** 12-18 months
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Should I raise venture capital?
    A: Probably not. Most micro-SaaS businesses don't need VC. If you want to grow faster, consider revenue-based financing or a small business loan instead.
  - Q: How do I find my first hire?
    A: Look for someone who uses your product and loves it. They already understand the customer. Hire for attitude first, skills second.

### From $25,000 to $50,000 MRR: Acquisition Target
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/milestones/25k-to-50k
- **Stage:** High Growth ($25,000-$50,000)
- **Summary:** At $50K MRR ($600K ARR), your business is an acquisition target. This is the sweet spot for MicroAcquire and bootstrapped SaaS buyers. Build for transferability.
- **Key Metrics:**
  - Monthly churn: <2%
  - Enterprise customers: >20% of revenue
  - Net revenue retention: >110%
  - Team size: 5-8 people
  - Valuation range: $1.5M-$3M
- **Tactics:**
  - Build for acquisition (High effort): Document all systems, reduce founder dependency, build a management team. Buyers pay more for businesses that don't need the founder.
  - Add a second product line (High effort): Expand with a complementary product. Cross-sell to existing customers. A second product doubles your TAM and your valuation multiple.
  - Develop strategic partnerships (Medium effort): Integrate with complementary platforms. Revenue-sharing partnerships can open distribution channels you can't access alone.
- **Common Mistakes:** Not starting the acquisition readiness process early enough; Founder being a bottleneck on every decision; Neglecting to diversify customer concentration risk
- **Time Estimate:** 12-24 months
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: What multiple can I expect in an acquisition?
    A: Bootstrapped SaaS businesses sell for 3-5x ARR. At $600K ARR with good growth and low churn, expect $1.8M-$3M. Add 1-2x for strategic buyers.
  - Q: Should I sell or keep growing?
    A: That's a personal question. If you love building, keep going. If you want liquidity or a new challenge, sell. Both paths are valid.

### From $50,000+ MRR: The Exit-Ready Business
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/milestones/50k-plus
- **Stage:** Exit Ready ($50,000+)
- **Summary:** You've built a $600K+ ARR business. You're in the top 1% of bootstrapped founders. Now it's about scale, systems, and optionality: acqui-hire, growth equity, or lifestyle business.
- **Key Metrics:**
  - Monthly churn: <2%
  - Net revenue retention: >120%
  - Annual recurring revenue: $600K+
  - Team size: 5-15 people
  - Valuation: $2M-$5M+
- **Tactics:**
  - Hire a CEO or COO (High effort): Step away from day-to-day operations. Hire someone to run the business. Keep your equity. Become the product visionary.
  - Explore secondary sales (High effort): Sell 10-20% of your equity to an investor or employee. Get liquidity without selling the whole company. Diversity your net worth.
  - Product-led growth engine (High effort): Your product should sell itself. Self-serve onboarding, viral loops, and community-driven growth should generate most new customers.
- **Common Mistakes:** Not building a management team early enough; Letting the business plateau instead of experimenting; Failing to diversify revenue across segments and geographies
- **Time Estimate:** 3-5 years total journey
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Is it worth staying at this stage or sell?
    A: If you love building, stay. If you want financial freedom, sell at 3-5x ARR. The 'right' answer depends on your goals, not the business metrics.
  - Q: How do I prevent the business from plateauing?
    A: Keep innovating. Don't optimize what exists at the expense of new experiments. Dedicate 20% of engineering to new product bets.

## Builder Timeline Pages

### Month 1: Idea + Validation
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/timeline/month-1
- **Month:** 1
- **Summary:** Month 1 is the most important. Don't build anything yet. Your goal is to have a validated idea and at least one person who's willing to pay. Everything else comes later.
- **Milestones:**
  - [✓] List 10 problems you've experienced or observed: Write down every business problem you've encountered. The best ideas come from your own experience.
  - [✓] Interview 5 potential customers: Find 5 people who have each problem. Ask about current solutions and frustrations. Do not pitch.
  - [○] Choose one problem to solve: Based on interviews, pick the problem that's most painful and most common.
  - [○] Write a one-page problem statement: Define the problem, who has it, and why existing solutions fail. 1 page maximum.
  - [○] Create a landing page with email capture: Build a simple page describing the solution. Collect emails. No product yet.
- **Metrics to Check:** Emails collected (target: 10+); Interview willingness (target: 80% say 'yes'); Problem pain level (target: 7+/10)
- **Common Mistakes:** Falling in love with your solution instead of the problem; Interviewing friends who will tell you what you want to hear; Picking an idea from 'cool tech' instead of painful problems
- **What's Next:** Month 2 is about pre-selling. If you can't get 3 people to pay before you build, go back to month 1 with a different idea.
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: How many hours per week should I spend in month 1?
    A: 5-10 hours. Most of it on customer conversations, not building. If you're spending more time coding than talking to people, you're moving too fast.
  - Q: What if I can't find people to interview?
    A: Then you're not in the right industry or you don't understand the problem deeply enough. Go where your target customers are: LinkedIn, Reddit, industry forums, professional networks.

### Month 3: MVP Launch
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/timeline/month-3
- **Month:** 3
- **Summary:** By month 3, you should have a functional MVP and your first paying customer. The goal is not perfection — it's a working solution that one person pays for.
- **Milestones:**
  - [✓] Pre-sell to at least 3 customers: Convince 3 people to pay before you build. If you can't, the problem isn't painful enough.
  - [✓] Build the MVP (one core feature only): Build only the single feature that solves the core problem. Resist scope creep fiercely.
  - [○] Launch to your pre-sold customers: Give your 3 pre-sold customers access. Get their feedback. Ship improvements daily.
  - [○] Set up analytics: Install product analytics. Track signups, activations, and daily usage from day one.
  - [○] Write welcome email sequence: Create a 3-email onboarding: welcome, setup guide, first win checklist.
- **Metrics to Check:** Topline MRR; Daily/weekly active users; Onboarding completion rate; Support tickets per day
- **Common Mistakes:** Adding features before the core workflow works; Polishing UI instead of testing the value proposition; Not charging enough (price is part of validation)
- **What's Next:** Month 4-6 is about getting to $500 MRR. Focus on retention, not acquisition. If your first customers stay, you have something worth building on.
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: What if my MVP has bugs?
    A: Ship it anyway. Early customers tolerate bugs if the value is clear. Fix bugs fast, but don't delay launch for perfection.
  - Q: How do I handle support in month 3?
    A: You ARE support. Every ticket is a product insight. Reply within 1 hour during business hours. Use issues as fuel for your roadmap.

### Month 6: First $500 MRR
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/timeline/month-6
- **Month:** 6
- **Summary:** Six months in, you should be at or approaching $500 MRR. The product is working for your early customers and you're starting to find repeatable acquisition patterns.
- **Milestones:**
  - [✓] Reach $500 MRR: 10-20 paying customers at $29/month or 5-10 at $49/month.
  - [✓] Find one repeatable acquisition channel: Identify where your last 5 customers came from. Double down on that channel.
  - [○] Reduce churn to under 10%: Analyze why customers leave. Fix the top 3 reasons. Reach out to every churned customer personally.
  - [○] Implement feedback loop: Create a system for collecting, prioritizing, and acting on customer feedback weekly.
  - [○] Write 5 blog posts: Answer the exact questions your customers Googled before finding you. Each post drives organic traffic.
- **Metrics to Check:** MRR trend (up or flat?); Churn rate (below 10%?); Customer acquisition cost; Net promoter score or customer satisfaction
- **Common Mistakes:** Building features when you should be selling; Neglecting churn analysis (losers disguise as 'busy'); Being everywhere instead of dominating one channel
- **What's Next:** Months 7-12 are about scaling to $2K+ MRR. Invest in content, referrals, and the product improvements that reduce churn.
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Is $500 MRR in 6 months good?
    A: Yes. Most micro-SaaS ideas die before reaching $500. You've proven demand. Now the question is whether you can scale it.
  - Q: Should I raise prices at $500 MRR?
    A: Not yet. Focus on getting more customers at your current price. Raise prices once you're at capacity or have clear evidence of underpricing.

### Month 12: $2K+ MRR
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/timeline/month-12
- **Month:** 12
- **Summary:** One year in, you should be at $2K+ MRR with a clear path to $4K/month (your freedom number). The business has momentum and you know what works.
- **Milestones:**
  - [✓] Reach $2K+ MRR: 40-70 paying customers. Steady growth trend.
  - [✓] Content engine running: 2-4 posts per month, 2K+ organic visits, consistent new signups from content.
  - [✓] Customer referral system in place: Referral program active. 10-20% of new customers come from referrals.
  - [○] Automated onboarding complete: New users can sign up, onboard, and get value without your involvement.
  - [○] Annual plans offered: 15%+ of customers on annual billing. Improves retention and cash flow.
- **Metrics to Check:** MRR growth rate (month over month); Churn rate (under 8%?); Referral source breakdown; Annual vs monthly revenue split
- **Common Mistakes:** Giving up too early (most $2K MRR businesses die before $4K); Hiring too soon (you don't have margin yet); Not raising prices (your product is worth more now)
- **What's Next:** Months 13-18 are the final push to $4K/month (freedom number). Focus on SEO, partnerships, and the features that unlock higher price tiers.
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Should I quit my job at $2K MRR?
    A: No. $2K MRR is ~$1.5K after taxes. That's not enough to replace your salary. Wait until you're at $4K MRR consistently for 3+ months.
  - Q: How do I break through the $2K plateau?
    A: Find one channel you've under-invested in and go all-in. Most people spread across 5 channels and get 10% from each. Pick one and get 80% from it.

### Month 18: The Freedom Number
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/timeline/month-18
- **Month:** 18
- **Summary:** Eighteen months is the target. You should be at $4K+ MRR with a business that runs well enough that quitting your job is a real, mathematical option.
- **Milestones:**
  - [✓] Reach $4K+ MRR (freedom number): 138+ customers at $29/month. Your side business covers core living expenses.
  - [✓] Business runs without you: Onboarding, support, and billing are automated. You could take a week off without revenue dropping.
  - [✓] Clear decision on job vs business: You know your number. You know your runway. The decision is psychological, not financial.
  - [✓] Systems and documentation in place: All processes documented. A contractor could run the business for 2 weeks.
  - [✓] Growth plan for post-job phase: Plan for what you'll do with full-time focus: double down on content, build second product, or enterprise sales.
- **Metrics to Check:** MRR stability (3+ months above $4K); Churn rate (under 5%?); Profit margin; Time you spend vs revenue generated
- **Common Mistakes:** Waiting for $10K MRR to quit (you don't need that); Not having a health insurance plan; Staying for 'one more bonus cycle' indefinitely
- **What's Next:** You've reached the freedom number. Now you decide: grow the business, start another, or take a breather. You've earned the right to choose.
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Is $4K MRR really enough to quit?
    A: For most single people in non-VHCOL areas, yes. $4K MRR = ~$3K after taxes. If your core expenses are under $3K, you have a decision to make.
  - Q: What if I want more before quitting?
    A: That's fine. Keep growing. But be honest — is it about financial safety or fear? Your business has customers who pay you every month. That's more predictable than any job.

## Profession Tool Stack Pages

### The Accountant's Micro-SaaS Tool Stack
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/stack/for-accountants
- **Profession:** Accountants
- **Total Monthly Cost:** $89/month (replaces $340K+ in team salaries)
- **Summary:** Accountants already understand compliance, billing cycles, and financial workflows. This is the exact tool stack that lets you turn that domain expertise into a profitable micro-SaaS — without writing code full-time or quitting your job.
- **Stack:**
  - AI / Coding: Claude Pro — Best for financial logic, compliance text, and formula generation. Understands accounting terminology. ($20/month)
  - AI / Coding: Cursor Pro — AI-powered IDE for building your SaaS without a developer. Writes and refactors code. ($20/month)
  - Payments: Stripe — Subscriptions, invoicing, and tax handling. Accountants already know Stripe from client work. (2.9% + 30¢/transaction)
  - Database: Supabase — Postgres database with auth and API. Free tier covers your first 50 customers. (Free → $25/month)
  - Analytics: PostHog — Track user behavior, funnel metrics, and churn. Self-hostable for privacy. (Free)
  - Hosting: Vercel — Deploy your SaaS in one click. Free tier handles moderate traffic. (Free → $20/month)
  - Email: Resend — Transaction emails for receipts, alerts, and onboarding sequences. (Free → $20/month)
  - Automation: Zapier — Connect QuickBooks, Xero, and your SaaS without writing integration code. (Free → $20/month)
- **Weekly Time:** 8-10 hours/week (2hr building, 4hr validating, 2hr distribution, 1hr admin)
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Do I need to know how to code?
    A: No. Claude Pro and Cursor Pro handle 80% of coding. You need to understand the logic and requirements — which accountants already do.
  - Q: Can I use QuickBooks or Xero APIs?
    A: Yes, but check your firm's policies first. Build for a different client segment than your employer serves. The Stripe and Plaid APIs are safer starting points.

### The Software Engineer's Micro-SaaS Tool Stack
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/stack/for-software-engineers
- **Profession:** Software Engineers
- **Total Monthly Cost:** $70/month (replaces Zero — you ARE the team)
- **Summary:** Software engineers have the technical skills — the challenge is scope, speed, and staying invisible. This stack maximizes what you can build in limited hours while keeping your side business separate from your employer.
- **Stack:**
  - AI / Coding: Cursor Pro — The best AI coding experience. Multi-file refactoring, inline completions, and codebase awareness. ($20/month)
  - AI / Coding: Claude Pro — For architecture decisions, code review, and writing copy/docs you'd rather not write yourself. ($20/month)
  - Payments: Stripe — Developer-first API, excellent docs, handles subscriptions and tax. (2.9% + 30¢/transaction)
  - Database: Supabase — Open-source Firebase alternative. Postgres, auth, real-time, storage. Generous free tier. (Free → $25/month)
  - Analytics: PostHog — Open-source product analytics. Event tracking, feature flags, session replay. (Free)
  - Hosting: Vercel — Edge network, automatic HTTPS, preview deployments. Perfect for Next.js/React. (Free → $20/month)
  - Monitoring: Sentry — Error tracking and performance monitoring. Free tier covers early stage. (Free → $26/month)
  - Domain: Cloudflare — DNS, CDN, DDoS protection. $10/year domain registration. ($10/year)
- **Weekly Time:** 6-8 hours/week (you build faster, so spend more time on distribution)
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: How do I avoid IP claims from my employer?
    A: Never use company equipment, code, or time. Build in a different domain than your employer. Use a personal laptop and separate accounts for everything.
  - Q: Should I open source my side project?
    A: Only if it drives distribution. For most micro-SaaS, closed-source is better — you're selling a service, not software. Open source周边工具 to build authority.

### The Product Manager's Micro-SaaS Tool Stack
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/stack/for-product-managers
- **Profession:** Product Managers
- **Total Monthly Cost:** $95/month (replaces 1 full-stack developer + 1 designer ($250K+/year))
- **Summary:** Product managers know what to build — the gap is execution speed. This stack bridges that gap with AI-powered tools that let you ship without a full engineering team. No coding bootcamp required.
- **Stack:**
  - AI / Coding: Cursor Pro — Build functional MVPs from specs. The closest thing to having a developer who executes your PRDs. ($20/month)
  - AI / Coding: Claude Pro — Write specs, user stories, and copy. Generate competitive analysis and feature prioritization. ($20/month)
  - No-Code: Bubble or Framer — Rapid prototyping and landing pages without touching code. Ship a testable MVP in a weekend. ($25-$32/month)
  - Design: Figma — Design mockups and wireframes. You already know this from your day job. (Free → $12/month)
  - Payments: Stripe — Payment links for pre-sales. Set up subscriptions in minutes. (2.9% + 30¢/transaction)
  - Analytics: PostHog — Funnel tracking and user behavior. PMs love data — this is your dashboard. (Free)
  - Hosting: Vercel — Deploy from GitHub in one click. Zero DevOps required. (Free → $20/month)
  - Research: Perplexity Pro — Market research with cited sources. Validate demand before building. ($20/month)
- **Weekly Time:** 8-10 hours/week (PMs need extra time on distribution since code is delegated to AI)
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can I use product management skills from my day job?
    A: Absolutely — spec writing, prioritization, and user research transfer directly. The risk is building too much before validating. Ship smaller.
  - Q: Should I hire a developer instead of using AI?
    A: Start with AI tools. If your SaaS reaches $2K+ MRR, then hire a contractor for complex features. AI handles 80% of early-stage code.

- **Total Monthly Cost:** $85/month (replaces 1 developer + 1 content team ($200K+/year))

### The Financial Analyst's Micro-SaaS Tool Stack

### The Nurse's Micro-SaaS Tool Stack
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/stack/for-nurses
- **Profession:** Nurses
- **Total Monthly Cost:** $70/month (replaces 1 developer ($120K+/year))
- **Summary:** Nurses understand healthcare workflows, patient communication, and clinical operations. This stack turns that frontline expertise into tools that clinics, patients, and other nurses will pay for.
- **Stack:**
  - AI / Coding: Claude Pro — Write health education content, generate care plans, and build patient communication tools. ($20/month)
  - AI / Coding: Cursor Pro — Build scheduling tools, patient trackers, and health calculators. ($20/month)
  - No-Code: Glide or Softr — Build mobile-friendly apps from spreadsheets. Perfect for patient-facing tools. (Free → $25/month)
  - Payments: Stripe — Charge for premium health tools, courses, or templates. (2.9% + 30¢/transaction)
  - Database: Airtable — Manage patient education content, care protocols, and scheduling data. (Free → $20/month)
  - Hosting: Vercel — Deploy HIPAA-conscious web apps (use a BAA for any PHI). (Free → $20/month)
- **Weekly Time:** 6-8 hours/week (use days off — nurses have flexible schedules)
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Do I need to worry about HIPAA?
    A: Only if you handle Protected Health Information (PHI). Build tools that don't store PHI, or use HIPAA-compliant infrastructure (Supabase with BAA, Azure with BAA).
  - Q: What's the best micro-SaaS for nurses?
    A: Tools that solve clinical workflow pain: shift scheduling, patient education generators, medication interaction checkers, or CEU tracking tools.

### The Teacher's Micro-SaaS Tool Stack
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/stack/for-teachers
- **Profession:** Teachers
- **Total Monthly Cost:** $65/month (replaces 1 developer + content team ($150K+/year))
- **Summary:** Teachers understand curriculum design, assessment, and learner psychology. This stack turns those skills into educational tools that students, parents, and other educators will pay for.
- **Stack:**
  - AI / Coding: Claude Pro — Generate lesson plans, quiz questions, and educational content at scale. ($20/month)
  - AI / Coding: Cursor Pro — Build quiz tools, flashcard apps, and grading automation. ($20/month)
  - No-Code: Glide — Build educational apps from spreadsheets. No coding required. (Free → $25/month)
  - Payments: Stripe + Gumroad — Sell digital products (worksheets, courses) and SaaS subscriptions. (2.9% + 30¢/transaction)
  - Content: Canva Pro — Create professional worksheets, presentations, and educational graphics. ($13/month)
  - Hosting: Vercel — Deploy educational web apps for free. (Free → $20/month)
- **Weekly Time:** 8-10 hours/week (use evenings, weekends, and summer breaks)
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can I sell materials I created for my classroom?
    A: Check your employment contract. Many districts claim IP rights to materials created during employment. Create new, original content outside of school hours and equipment.
  - Q: What's the best micro-SaaS for teachers?
    A: Tools for other educators: quiz generators, lesson plan templates, parent communication tools, or student progress trackers. You know the pain points.

## Cost of Waiting Pages

### The Cost of Waiting 1 Year at $80K/Year
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/cost-of-waiting/1-years-$80k-salary
- **Salary:** $80K | **Horizon:** 1-year
- **Salary Earned:** $80,000
- **Micro-SaaS Revenue (if started):** $21,314
- **Opportunity Cost:** $58,686
- **Compounded Loss:** $31,970
- **Summary:** You earn $80K/year. Over 1 year, that's $80K in salary. But if you'd started building a micro-SaaS 1 year ago, you'd have $21K in additional recurring revenue. Here's the breakdown.
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: What does $80K/year translate to monthly?
    A: $80K/year = $6,667/month before taxes. After federal, state, and FICA taxes, take-home is approximately $4,533/month depending on your state.
  - Q: How long does it take to replace a $80K salary with micro-SaaS?
    A: At $80K/year, your freedom number is approximately $4,667/month in net MRR. With a micro-SaaS charging $29/month, that's 161 customers. Most founders reach this in 14-20 months.
  - Q: What if I'd started 1 year ago?
    A: If you'd started 1 year ago with consistent effort (5-8 hours/week), you'd have accumulated approximately $21K in total micro-SaaS revenue. That's recurring revenue that continues paying you every month — unlike salary, which stops when you stop working.

- **Salary Earned:** $600,000
- **Micro-SaaS Revenue (if started):** $532,161
- **Opportunity Cost:** $67,839
- **Compounded Loss:** $798,241
- **Summary:** You earn $120K/year. Over 5 years, that's $600K in salary. But if you'd started building a micro-SaaS 5 years ago, you'd have $532K in additional recurring revenue. Here's the breakdown.
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: What does $120K/year translate to monthly?
    A: $120K/year = $10,000/month before taxes. After federal, state, and FICA taxes, take-home is approximately $6,800/month depending on your state.
  - Q: How long does it take to replace a $120K salary with micro-SaaS?
    A: At $120K/year, your freedom number is approximately $7,000/month in net MRR. With a micro-SaaS charging $29/month, that's 242 customers. Most founders reach this in 14-20 months.
  - Q: What if I'd started 5 years ago?
    A: If you'd started 5 years ago with consistent effort (5-8 hours/week), you'd have accumulated approximately $532K in total micro-SaaS revenue. That's recurring revenue that continues paying you every month — unlike salary, which stops when you stop working.

## Micro-SaaS Ideas by Profession × State

### Micro-SaaS Ideas for Software Engineers in California
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/ideas/for-software-engineers/in/california
- **Profession:** Software Engineers | **State:** California
- **Summary:** Engineers can build, ship, and maintain products without hiring anyone — the ultimate unfair advantage in micro-SaaS. In California, non-competes are essentially void in California (Banned except for sale-of-business and partnership exit scenarios.). LLC formation costs $70 plus a $20 annual report fee, and California's income tax rate is 1-13.3%.
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can a software engineer in California build a SaaS while employed?
    A: Yes — and California is one of the best states to do it. Non-competes are essentially void, so your employer cannot use a restrictive covenant to block your side business. Do not use your employer's code, infrastructure, or IP. Build in a completely different tech stack or domain. Check your employment agreement for IP assignment clauses. Form an LLC ($70 in California) to separate your side-business liability, and use a personal device for all entrepreneurial work.
  - Q: What does it cost to form an LLC in California as a software engineer?
    A: Forming an LLC in California costs $70. The annual report costs $20. California's income tax rate is 1-13.3%, which applies to your SaaS profits on top of federal and self-employment taxes. For software engineers earning $120K-$300K, the LLC structure provides liability protection and keeps your side-business income separate from your W-2 employment.

### Micro-SaaS Ideas for Software Engineers in Texas
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/ideas/for-software-engineers/in/texas
- **Profession:** Software Engineers | **State:** Texas
- **Summary:** Engineers can build, ship, and maintain products without hiring anyone — the ultimate unfair advantage in micro-SaaS. In Texas, non-competes are enforceable in Texas (Enforced if reasonable and protecting legitimate interests.). LLC formation costs $300 with no annual report required, and Texas levies no state income tax, letting you keep more of your SaaS revenue.
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can a software engineer in Texas build a SaaS while employed?
    A: Yes, but proceed carefully. Texas enforces non-competes (enforced if reasonable and protecting legitimate interests.). The key rule: do not build a product that directly competes with your employer, and never use company time, devices, or data. Do not use your employer's code, infrastructure, or IP. Build in a completely different tech stack or domain. Check your employment agreement for IP assignment clauses. Form an LLC ($300 in Texas) to separate your side-business liability, and use a personal device for all entrepreneurial work.
  - Q: What does it cost to form an LLC in Texas as a software engineer?
    A: Forming an LLC in Texas costs $300. Texas does not require an annual report, keeping ongoing costs near zero. Texas has no state income tax, so your SaaS profits are only subject to federal tax plus the 15.3% self-employment tax. For software engineers earning $120K-$300K, the LLC structure provides liability protection and keeps your side-business income separate from your W-2 employment.

The key rule: do not build a product that directly competes with your employer, and never use company time, devices, or data. Do not use your employer's code, infrastructure, or IP. Build in a completely different tech stack or domain. Check your employment agreement for IP assignment clauses. Form an LLC ($50 in Michigan) to separate your side-business liability, and use a personal device for all entrepreneurial work.
  - Q: What does it cost to form an LLC in Michigan as a software engineer?
    A: Forming an LLC in Michigan costs $50. The annual report costs $25. Michigan's income tax rate is 4.25%, which applies to your SaaS profits on top of federal and self-employment taxes. For software engineers earning $120K-$300K, the LLC structure provides liability protection and keeps your side-business income separate from your W-2 employment.

Form an LLC ($100 in Virginia) to separate your side-business liability, and use a personal device for all entrepreneurial work.
  - Q: What does it cost to form an LLC in Virginia as a management consultant?
    A: Forming an LLC in Virginia costs $100. The annual report costs $50. Virginia's income tax rate is 2-5.75%, which applies to your SaaS profits on top of federal and self-employment taxes. For management consultants earning $150K-$300K, the LLC structure provides liability protection and keeps your side-business income separate from your W-2 employment.

Do not practice law through your SaaS (unauthorized practice of law). Build tools that assist non-lawyers or streamline firm operations, not legal advice engines. Form an LLC ($70 in California) to separate your side-business liability, and use a personal device for all entrepreneurial work.

The key rule: do not build a product that directly competes with your employer, and never use company time, devices, or data. Do not use your employer's candidate database or client lists. Build tools that help recruiters work with their own sourced candidates. Form an LLC ($100 in Georgia) to separate your side-business liability, and use a personal device for all entrepreneurial work.
  - Q: What does it cost to form an LLC in Georgia as a recruiter?
    A: Forming an LLC in Georgia costs $100. The annual report costs $50. Georgia's income tax rate is 1-5.49%, which applies to your SaaS profits on top of federal and self-employment taxes. For recruiters earning $60K-$150K, the LLC structure provides liability protection and keeps your side-business income separate from your W-2 employment.

    A: Yes, but proceed carefully. Florida enforces non-competes (strongly enforced under florida's specific statutory framework.). The key rule: do not build a product that directly competes with your employer, and never use company time, devices, or data. Never provide medical advice through your SaaS without proper licensing and liability insurance. Build tools for workflow, education, or compliance — not diagnosis or treatment. Form an LLC ($125 in Florida) to separate your side-business liability, and use a personal device for all entrepreneurial work.
  - Q: What does it cost to form an LLC in Florida as a physician?
    A: Forming an LLC in Florida costs $125. The annual report costs $138.75. Florida has no state income tax, so your SaaS profits are only subject to federal tax plus the 15.3% self-employment tax. For physicians earning $200K-$500K, the LLC structure provides liability protection and keeps your side-business income separate from your W-2 employment.

## Non-Compete Risk Matrix

### Non-Compete Guide for Software Engineers in California
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/non-compete/software-engineers-california
- **Profession:** Software Engineers | **State:** California
- **Can Build:** Yes
- **Enforceability:** Non-competes are essentially void in California (Banned except for sale-of-business and partnership exit scenarios.)
- **Key Risks:** Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted; Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines; GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes; California's $800 annual LLC franchise tax applies regardless of profit; Invention assignment clauses may still be enforceable even though non-competes are void
- **Safe Harbors:** Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's; Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources; Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there; Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code §16600 voids non-competes — the strongest protection in the U.S.; Labor Code §2870 protects employee inventions created on personal time with personal resources (with exceptions for employer's business)
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can a software engineer in California start a side business?
    A: Yes. Non-competes are essentially void in California (Banned except for sale-of-business and partnership exit scenarios.). You face no enforceable non-compete barrier to building a micro-SaaS in California. However, you must still avoid using employer trade secrets, client data, or resources. No profession-specific non-compete shield, but software engineers are the most common targets of invention assignment enforcement. Focus on these safe practices: Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's.
  - Q: What non-compete risks should software engineers in California watch for?
    A: The top risks for software engineers in California are:
  1. Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted
  2. Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines
  3. GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes
  4. California's $800 annual LLC franchise tax applies regardless of profit

To protect yourself: Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there

### Non-Compete Guide for Software Engineers in Texas
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/non-compete/software-engineers-texas
- **Profession:** Software Engineers | **State:** Texas
- **Can Build:** Yes
- **Enforceability:** Non-competes are enforceable in Texas (Enforced if reasonable and protecting legitimate interests.)
- **Key Risks:** Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted; Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines; GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes; Texas enforces non-competes that are ancillary to an otherwise enforceable agreement and reasonable in scope, geography, and duration (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code §15.50); Recent Texas reforms (2023-2025) have tightened reasonableness standards but enforcement remains strong for high earners
- **Safe Harbors:** Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's; Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources; Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there; Texas requires the non-compete to be tied to an enforceable agreement (usually a confidentiality or non-solicit clause) — if that underlying agreement fails, the non-compete may also fail; No state income tax means your SaaS profits are not taxed at the state level
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can a software engineer in Texas start a side business?
    A: Yes, but you must structure your side business carefully. Non-competes are enforceable in Texas (Enforced if reasonable and protecting legitimate interests.). The critical rules: (1) do not build a product that directly competes with your employer, (2) never use company time, devices, data, or resources, and (3) review your employment agreement's non-compete and IP assignment clauses before launching. No profession-specific non-compete shield, but software engineers are the most common targets of invention assignment enforcement.
  - Q: What non-compete risks should software engineers in Texas watch for?
    A: The top risks for software engineers in Texas are:
  1. Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted
  2. Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines
  3. GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes
  4. Texas enforces non-competes that are ancillary to an otherwise enforceable agreement and reasonable in scope, geography, and duration (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code §15.50)

To protect yourself: Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there

### Non-Compete Guide for Software Engineers in New York

- **Profession:** Software Engineers | **State:** New York

- **Enforceability:** Non-compete enforcement is limited in New York (Allowed if reasonable; recent reforms restrict low-wage use.)
- **Key Risks:** Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted; Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines; GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes; NY courts enforce non-competes if reasonable in duration, geography, and scope, though recent reforms (2023+) restrict use for lower-wage workers; NYC has additional worker protection laws that may limit enforceability within the five boroughs
- **Safe Harbors:** Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's; Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources; Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there; NY requires employer consideration (continued employment alone may not suffice for existing employees); Recent NY legislation restricts non-competes for workers earning below a threshold (check current limits); Courts apply 'blue pencil' doctrine — may modify rather than void overly broad agreements
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can a software engineer in New York start a side business?
    A: Yes, with meaningful protections. Non-compete enforcement is limited in New York (Allowed if reasonable; recent reforms restrict low-wage use.). Even though some restrictions exist, New York's limits provide significant room to build a side business if you avoid direct competition with your employer. No profession-specific non-compete shield, but software engineers are the most common targets of invention assignment enforcement.   - Q: What non-compete risks should software engineers in New York watch for?
    A: The top risks for software engineers in New York are:
  1. Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted
  2. Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines
  3. GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes
  4. NY courts enforce non-competes if reasonable in duration, geography, and scope, though recent reforms (2023+) restrict use for lower-wage workers

### Non-Compete Guide for Software Engineers in Florida
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/non-compete/software-engineers-florida
- **Profession:** Software Engineers | **State:** Florida
- **Can Build:** Yes
- **Enforceability:** Non-competes are enforceable in Florida (Strongly enforced under Florida's specific statutory framework.)
- **Key Risks:** Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted; Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines; GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes; Florida strongly enforces non-competes under Fla. Stat. §542.335 — courts routinely uphold reasonable restrictions; Florida's statutory framework presumes enforceability if the employer shows a legitimate business interest
- **Safe Harbors:** Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's; Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources; Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there; No state income tax reduces the financial burden of side-business operations; Fla. Stat. §542.335 requires the employer to plead and prove a legitimate business interest — vague claims may not survive; Florida courts will reduce overly broad restrictions rather than void the entire agreement (reformation)
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can a software engineer in Florida start a side business?
    A: Yes, but you must structure your side business carefully. Non-competes are enforceable in Florida (Strongly enforced under Florida's specific statutory framework.). The critical rules: (1) do not build a product that directly competes with your employer, (2) never use company time, devices, data, or resources, and (3) review your employment agreement's non-compete and IP assignment clauses before launching. No profession-specific non-compete shield, but software engineers are the most common targets of invention assignment enforcement.
  - Q: What non-compete risks should software engineers in Florida watch for?
    A: The top risks for software engineers in Florida are:
  1. Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted
  2. Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines
  3. GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes
  4. Florida strongly enforces non-competes under Fla. Stat. §542.335 — courts routinely uphold reasonable restrictions

To protect yourself: Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there

- **Profession:** Software Engineers | **State:** Illinois
- **Can Build:** Yes
- **Enforceability:** Non-compete enforcement is limited in Illinois (Banned for workers earning under $75K (2023 reform).)
- **Key Risks:** Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted; Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines; GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes; Illinois enforces non-competes for workers earning above $75K/year (Freedom to Work Act, 2023 reform); The $75K threshold increases annually — verify current limits before relying on it
- **Safe Harbors:** Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's; Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources; Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there; Illinois bans non-competes for workers earning under $75K/year (rising to $90K by 2037) — covers many professionals; The Freedom to Work Act requires employers to advise employees in writing to consult an attorney before signing; Illinois courts apply strict 'reasonableness' scrutiny to duration and geographic scope
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can a software engineer in Illinois start a side business?
    A: Yes, with meaningful protections. Non-compete enforcement is limited in Illinois (Banned for workers earning under $75K (2023 reform).). Even though some restrictions exist, Illinois's limits provide significant room to build a side business if you avoid direct competition with your employer. No profession-specific non-compete shield, but software engineers are the most common targets of invention assignment enforcement.   - Q: What non-compete risks should software engineers in Illinois watch for?
    A: The top risks for software engineers in Illinois are:
  1. Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted
  2. Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines
  3. GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes
  4. Illinois enforces non-competes for workers earning above $75K/year (Freedom to Work Act, 2023 reform)

To protect yourself: Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there

- **Safe Harbors:** Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's; Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources; Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there; Non-competes are void for workers below the ~$120K earnings threshold (RCW 49.62); WA law requires garden leave compensation if the employer enforces a non-compete post-termination; No personal wage income tax (7% capital gains tax applies to investment gains over ~$270K)
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can a software engineer in Washington start a side business?
    A: Yes, with meaningful protections. Non-compete enforcement is limited in Washington (Restricted for workers earning under ~$120K/year.). No profession-specific non-compete shield, but software engineers are the most common targets of invention assignment enforcement.   - Q: What non-compete risks should software engineers in Washington watch for?
    A: The top risks for software engineers in Washington are:
  1. Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted
  2. Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines
  3. GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes
  4. Washington enforces non-competes for workers earning above approximately $120K/year (threshold adjusts annually)

To protect yourself: Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there

### Non-Compete Guide for Software Engineers in Georgia
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/non-compete/software-engineers-georgia
- **Profession:** Software Engineers | **State:** Georgia
- **Can Build:** Yes
- **Enforceability:** Non-competes are enforceable in Georgia (Enforced under Georgia's restrictive covenant statute if reasonable.)
- **Key Risks:** Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted; Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines; GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes; Georgia's Restrictive Covenants Act (O.C.G.A. §13-8-50 et seq., effective 2011) provides a favorable framework for employers to enforce non-competes; Georgia courts can 'blue pencil' (modify) overly broad agreements to make them enforceable rather than voiding them
- **Safe Harbors:** Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's; Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources; Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there; Georgia courts apply reasonableness tests to duration and scope — 2-year limits are typical; The 2011 Act requires non-competes to protect legitimate business interests, providing some defense if the interest is speculative
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can a software engineer in Georgia start a side business?
    A: Yes, but you must structure your side business carefully. Non-competes are enforceable in Georgia (Enforced under Georgia's restrictive covenant statute if reasonable.). The critical rules: (1) do not build a product that directly competes with your employer, (2) never use company time, devices, data, or resources, and (3) review your employment agreement's non-compete and IP assignment clauses before launching. No profession-specific non-compete shield, but software engineers are the most common targets of invention assignment enforcement.
  - Q: What non-compete risks should software engineers in Georgia watch for?
    A: The top risks for software engineers in Georgia are:
  1. Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted
  2. Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines
  3. GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes
  4. Georgia's Restrictive Covenants Act (O.C.G.A. §13-8-50 et seq., effective 2011) provides a favorable framework for employers to enforce non-competes

To protect yourself: Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there

### Non-Compete Guide for Software Engineers in Massachusetts
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/non-compete/software-engineers-massachusetts
- **Profession:** Software Engineers | **State:** Massachusetts
- **Can Build:** Yes
- **Enforceability:** Non-compete enforcement is limited in Massachusetts (Allowed under the 2018 MA Noncompetition Agreement Act with strict rules.)
- **Key Risks:** Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted; Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines; GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes; MA enforces non-competes under the 2018 MA Noncompetition Agreement Act (M.G.L. c. 149, §24L) with strict requirements but continued enforceability; MA requires 'garden leave' compensation (50% of salary) or other mutually agreed consideration during the restricted period; The $500 LLC filing fee is among the highest in the U.S.
- **Safe Harbors:** Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's; Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources; Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there; MA non-competes are limited to 12 months (with narrow exceptions for trade secret theft); Non-competes are void for non-exempt employees, students, and low-wage workers (under the MA minimum salary threshold); MA requires 10 business days advance notice before an employee signs, and the agreement must advise consulting an attorney
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can a software engineer in Massachusetts start a side business?
    A: Yes, with meaningful protections. Non-compete enforcement is limited in Massachusetts (Allowed under the 2018 MA Noncompetition Agreement Act with strict rules.). Even though some restrictions exist, Massachusetts's limits provide significant room to build a side business if you avoid direct competition with your employer. No profession-specific non-compete shield, but software engineers are the most common targets of invention assignment enforcement. Key precaution: Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted
  - Q: What non-compete risks should software engineers in Massachusetts watch for?
    A: The top risks for software engineers in Massachusetts are:
  1. Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted
  2. Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines
  3. GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes
  4. MA enforces non-competes under the 2018 MA Noncompetition Agreement Act (M.G.L. c. 149, §24L) with strict requirements but continued enforceability

To protect yourself: Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there

### Non-Compete Guide for Software Engineers in Colorado
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/non-compete/software-engineers-colorado
- **Profession:** Software Engineers | **State:** Colorado
- **Can Build:** Yes
- **Enforceability:** Non-competes are essentially void in Colorado (Void for workers earning under $100K/year (2022 reform).)
- **Key Risks:** Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted; Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines; GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes; Colorado voids non-competes for workers earning under $100K/year (C.R.S. §8-2-113, 2022 reform) but enforces them for higher earners; The $100K threshold adjusts annually for inflation
- **Safe Harbors:** Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's; Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources; Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there; Non-competes are void for workers below the $100K earnings threshold — protects the majority of professionals; Colorado requires non-competes to protect trade secrets and be no broader than necessary; Annual LLC report costs only $10 — among the cheapest in the U.S.
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can a software engineer in Colorado start a side business?
    A: Yes. Non-competes are essentially void in Colorado (Void for workers earning under $100K/year (2022 reform).). You face no enforceable non-compete barrier to building a micro-SaaS in Colorado. However, you must still avoid using employer trade secrets, client data, or resources. No profession-specific non-compete shield, but software engineers are the most common targets of invention assignment enforcement. Focus on these safe practices: Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's.
  - Q: What non-compete risks should software engineers in Colorado watch for?
    A: The top risks for software engineers in Colorado are:
  1. Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted
  2. Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines
  3. GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes
  4. Colorado voids non-competes for workers earning under $100K/year (C.R.S. §8-2-113, 2022 reform) but enforces them for higher earners

To protect yourself: Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there

### Non-Compete Guide for Software Engineers in North Carolina
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/non-compete/software-engineers-north-carolina
- **Profession:** Software Engineers | **State:** North Carolina
- **Can Build:** Yes
- **Enforceability:** Non-competes are enforceable in North Carolina (Enforced if reasonable in scope and duration.)
- **Key Risks:** Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted; Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines; GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes; NC enforces non-competes if reasonable in scope, geography, and duration, with no statutory income threshold; NC courts tend to enforce non-competes more readily than courts in reform states
- **Safe Harbors:** Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's; Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources; Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there; NC requires the employer to show a legitimate business interest (trade secrets, customer relationships, specialized training); Courts apply strict reasonableness review — overly broad restrictions may be struck entirely (NC does not always blue-pencil); Non-competes must be supported by adequate consideration (continued employment alone is debated)
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can a software engineer in North Carolina start a side business?
    A: Yes, but you must structure your side business carefully. Non-competes are enforceable in North Carolina (Enforced if reasonable in scope and duration.). The critical rules: (1) do not build a product that directly competes with your employer, (2) never use company time, devices, data, or resources, and (3) review your employment agreement's non-compete and IP assignment clauses before launching. No profession-specific non-compete shield, but software engineers are the most common targets of invention assignment enforcement.
  - Q: What non-compete risks should software engineers in North Carolina watch for?
    A: The top risks for software engineers in North Carolina are:
  1. Invention assignment clauses may claim ownership of code you write on personal time if broadly drafted
  2. Using familiar code patterns or libraries from work can blur IP ownership lines
  3. GitHub commit history on employer devices can be subpoenaed in disputes
  4. NC enforces non-competes if reasonable in scope, geography, and duration, with no statutory income threshold

To protect yourself: Build on a separate personal device using a different tech stack than your employer's Document your personal-time development with timestamps and personal resources Review your invention assignment agreement for the 'pre-existing inventions' schedule — list your side project there

### Non-Compete Guide for Product Managers in California
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/non-compete/product-managers-california
- **Profession:** Product Managers | **State:** California
- **Can Build:** Yes
- **Enforceability:** Non-competes are essentially void in California (Banned except for sale-of-business and partnership exit scenarios.)
- **Key Risks:** Knowledge of employer's product roadmap and competitive strategy is considered proprietary; Building tools for the same user segment as your employer risks trade secret claims; PMs often have access to customer data and feedback that can inadvertently inform side projects; California's $800 annual LLC franchise tax applies regardless of profit; Invention assignment clauses may still be enforceable even though non-competes are void
- **Safe Harbors:** Target a completely different customer segment or industry than your employer; Never reference or reuse internal strategy documents, roadmaps, or competitive analysis; Use no-code tools or partner with a developer rather than using employer engineering resources; Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code §16600 voids non-competes — the strongest protection in the U.S.; Labor Code §2870 protects employee inventions created on personal time with personal resources (with exceptions for employer's business)
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can a product manager in California start a side business?
    A: Yes. Non-competes are essentially void in California (Banned except for sale-of-business and partnership exit scenarios.). You face no enforceable non-compete barrier to building a micro-SaaS in California. However, you must still avoid using employer trade secrets, client data, or resources. PMs face fewer invention assignment risks than engineers but greater trade secret exposure due to broad information access. Focus on these safe practices: Target a completely different customer segment or industry than your employer.
  - Q: What non-compete risks should product managers in California watch for?
    A: The top risks for product managers in California are:
  1. Knowledge of employer's product roadmap and competitive strategy is considered proprietary
  2. Building tools for the same user segment as your employer risks trade secret claims
  3. PMs often have access to customer data and feedback that can inadvertently inform side projects
  4. California's $800 annual LLC franchise tax applies regardless of profit

To protect yourself: Target a completely different customer segment or industry than your employer Never reference or reuse internal strategy documents, roadmaps, or competitive analysis Use no-code tools or partner with a developer rather than using employer engineering resources

### Non-Compete Guide for Product Managers in Texas
- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/non-compete/product-managers-texas
- **Profession:** Product Managers | **State:** Texas
- **Can Build:** Yes
- **Enforceability:** Non-competes are enforceable in Texas (Enforced if reasonable and protecting legitimate interests.)
- **Key Risks:** Knowledge of employer's product roadmap and competitive strategy is considered proprietary; Building tools for the same user segment as your employer risks trade secret claims; PMs often have access to customer data and feedback that can inadvertently inform side projects; Texas enforces non-competes that are ancillary to an otherwise enforceable agreement and reasonable in scope, geography, and duration (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code §15.50); Recent Texas reforms (2023-2025) have tightened reasonableness standards but enforcement remains strong for high earners
- **Safe Harbors:** Target a completely different customer segment or industry than your employer; Never reference or reuse internal strategy documents, roadmaps, or competitive analysis; Use no-code tools or partner with a developer rather than using employer engineering resources; Texas requires the non-compete to be tied to an enforceable agreement (usually a confidentiality or non-solicit clause) — if that underlying agreement fails, the non-compete may also fail; No state income tax means your SaaS profits are not taxed at the state level
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can a product manager in Texas start a side business?
    A: Yes, but you must structure your side business carefully. Non-competes are enforceable in Texas (Enforced if reasonable and protecting legitimate interests.). The critical rules: (1) do not build a product that directly competes with your employer, (2) never use company time, devices, data, or resources, and (3) review your employment agreement's non-compete and IP assignment clauses before launching. PMs face fewer invention assignment risks than engineers but greater trade secret exposure due to broad information access.
  - Q: What non-compete risks should product managers in Texas watch for?
    A: The top risks for product managers in Texas are:
  1. Knowledge of employer's product roadmap and competitive strategy is considered proprietary
  2. Building tools for the same user segment as your employer risks trade secret claims
  3. PMs often have access to customer data and feedback that can inadvertently inform side projects
  4. Texas enforces non-competes that are ancillary to an otherwise enforceable agreement and reasonable in scope, geography, and duration (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code §15.50)

To protect yourself: Target a completely different customer segment or industry than your employer Never reference or reuse internal strategy documents, roadmaps, or competitive analysis Use no-code tools or partner with a developer rather than using employer engineering resources

- **URL:** https://invisibleexit.com/non-compete/product-managers-florida
- **Profession:** Product Managers | **State:** Florida
- **Can Build:** Yes
- **Enforceability:** Non-competes are enforceable in Florida (Strongly enforced under Florida's specific statutory framework.)
- **Key Risks:** Knowledge of employer's product roadmap and competitive strategy is considered proprietary; Building tools for the same user segment as your employer risks trade secret claims; PMs often have access to customer data and feedback that can inadvertently inform side projects; Florida strongly enforces non-competes under Fla. Stat. §542.335 — courts routinely uphold reasonable restrictions; Florida's statutory framework presumes enforceability if the employer shows a legitimate business interest
- **Safe Harbors:** Target a completely different customer segment or industry than your employer; Never reference or reuse internal strategy documents, roadmaps, or competitive analysis; Use no-code tools or partner with a developer rather than using employer engineering resources; No state income tax reduces the financial burden of side-business operations; Fla. Stat. §542.335 requires the employer to plead and prove a legitimate business interest — vague claims may not survive; Florida courts will reduce overly broad restrictions rather than void the entire agreement (reformation)
- **FAQs:**
  - Q: Can a product manager in Florida start a side business?
    A: Yes, but you must structure your side business carefully. Non-competes are enforceable in Florida (Strongly enforced under Florida's specific statutory framework.). The critical rules: (1) do not build a product that directly competes with your employer, (2) never use company time, devices, data, or resources, and (3) review your employment agreement's non-compete and IP assignment clauses before launching. PMs face fewer invention assignment risks than engineers but greater trade secret exposure due to broad information access.
  - Q: What non-compete risks should product managers in Florida watch for?
    A: The top risks for product managers in Florida are:
  1. Knowledge of employer's product roadmap and competitive strategy is considered proprietary
  2. Building tools for the same user segment as your employer risks trade secret claims
  3. PMs often have access to customer data and feedback that can inadvertently inform side projects
  4. Florida strongly enforces non-competes under Fla. Stat. §542.335 — courts routinely uphold reasonable restrictions

To protect yourself: Target a completely different customer segment or industry than your employer Never reference or reuse internal strategy documents, roadmaps, or competitive analysis Use no-code tools or partner with a developer rather than using employer engineering resources

- **Safe Harbors:** Target a completely different customer segment or industry than your employer; Never reference or reuse internal strategy documents, roadmaps, or competitive analysis; Use no-code tools or partner with a developer rather than using employer engineering resources; Illinois bans non-competes for workers earning under $75K/year (rising to $90K by 2037) — covers many professionals; The Freedom to Work Act requires employers to advise employees in writing to consult an attorney before signing; Illinois courts apply strict 'reasonableness' scrutiny to duration and geographic scope
